name: Arrhenius temperature dependence of rate constants
conventions: k is the rate constant (units depend on reaction order)
A is the pre-exponential factor (same units as k); empirical or calculated from collision theory / TST
E_a is the activation energy in J/mol (use kJ/mol with care — convert before substituting)
R is the universal gas constant: R = 8.31446 J/(mol K)
T in kelvin (absolute temperature, never deg C)
valid over moderate temperature ranges where E_a is roughly T-independent; modified Arrhenius forms (k = A T^n exp(-E_a / RT)) extend the range
expectedLimits: T -> 0:  k -> 0  (no thermal energy to surmount the barrier)
T -> infinity:  k -> A  (every collision crosses the barrier)
ln k vs 1/T is linear with slope -E_a / R  (Arrhenius plot — operational E_a definition)
rule of thumb: rate doubles for every ~10 K increase near room temperature when E_a ~ 50 kJ/mol
near-zero E_a: weakly temperature-dependent (radical recombination, diffusion-limited reactions)
very large E_a (>> RT): strongly temperature-dependent, tunnelling effects may dominate at low T
references: Arrhenius (1889) — Über die Reaktionsgeschwindigkeit bei der Inversion von Rohrzucker durch Säuren
IUPAC Gold Book — entry: Arrhenius equation
